title: See the Odd-Looking Barreleye Fish in Its Natural Habitat for the First Time
author: Jake Currie
text: Subscribe to skip adsAdvertisementZoologySee the Odd-Looking Barreleye Fish in Its Natural Habitat for the First TimeA 35-day expedition revealed hidden wonders of the deepJCBy Jake Currie9:31 AM CDT on July 10, 2026Share on FacebookShare on X (formerly Twitter)Share on RedditShare on EmailShare on BlueskyDeep beneath the ocean where the African and South American tectonic plates meet lies the Doldrums Fracture Zone. Judging by its name alone, you might guess it’s not a particularly interesting area of the ocean floor, but a recent scientific expedition to the region revealed it’s anything but boring.Subscribe to skip adsFeatured VideoPowered By 10 Strangest Eating Habits in the Wild! You Have to Check This OutShareNextStayScientists from the Schmidt Ocean Institute just completed a 35-day trip to the zone, located 800 miles off the northeast coast of Brazil, and discovered two new hydrothermal vent fields, teeming with bizarre-looking life-forms. Ghostly anemones, hungry crabs, and blind shrimp clustered around the rocky vents, feeding on specialized chemosynthetic bacteria. Far from the light of the sun, these bacteria rely on chemicals from the vents for their energy, forming the foundation of an alien-like ecosystem unlike anything on the surface. HIDDEN ECOSYSTEM: Life in the Doldrums Fracture Zone, documented by submersibles from the Schmidt Ocean Institute.
